refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss threshold during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the quality/reliability obligation as a single atomic requirement, expressed in the problem domain (what reliability must be achieved) without prescribing how (e.g., redundancy, error correction, network selection). The phrase 'communication quality' is replaced with the measurable, customer-defined metric 'packet loss threshold' to eliminate ambiguity. 'Agreed network conditions' anchors the requirement to realistic operational context rather than ideal conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions across the agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profiles, congestion scenarios) and measure end-to-end packet loss using network monitoring tools. Confirm that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%) over a statistically significant sample of operations.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of heavy equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback transmission with packet loss not exceeding the customer-defined threshold under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and forward error correction (FEC) with dynamic threshold adjustment based on real-time network quality metrics
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover and load balancing across independent network routes
DP3: Deploy a buffering and retransmission protocol with exponential backoff and selective acknowledgment (SACK) tuned to agreed network latency bounds
DP4: Implement application-level heartbeat monitoring with predictive congestion detection and graceful degradation of non-critical teleoperation data streams
DP5: Establish a hybrid approach combining packet prioritization (QoS tagging), local caching of critical control states, and network condition-aware codec adaptation
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Establish a hybrid approach combining packet prioritization (QoS tagging), local caching of critical control states, and network condition-aware codec adaptation

potential_sources: Coupling between QoS tagging logic and network stack implementation (mitigated by defining QoS as a logical contract, not a specific protocol)
Coupling between state caching and control command sequencing (mitigated by explicit state versioning and idempotency rules)
Coupling between codec adaptation and teleoperation command frequency (mitigated by defining adaptation thresholds as tunable parameters, not hard-coded)
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids tight coupling to specific transport protocols or vendor solutions. It decomposes into three semi-independent logical concerns (prioritization, caching, adaptation) that can be realized in different implementation modules without forcing architectural changes. The approach supports both physical network redundancy (DP2-like) and algorithmic resilience (DP1-like) without mandating either.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: 'Agreed network conditions' are formally specified with measurable parameters (latency, jitter, baseline packet loss rate, bandwidth). RISK: If conditions are vague, acceptance testing will be ambiguous.

ASSUMPTION: The teleoperation system can tolerate temporary state inconsistency during packet loss events (e.g., operator sees stale sensor data briefly). RISK: If real-time safety-critical feedback is required with zero tolerance for staleness, DP5 may require additional synchronization overhead.

ASSUMPTION: Local caching of critical control states does not violate safety interlocks or operational policies. RISK: Cached state could mask network failures if not properly monitored; requires explicit safety review.

ASSUMPTION: Network condition monitoring (latency, loss estimation) is available at the application layer or via OS-level APIs. RISK: If monitoring is unavailable or unreliable, codec adaptation and threshold tuning will fail.

ASSUMPTION: The customer-defined packet loss threshold (≤1%) is achievable under agreed conditions without requiring infrastructure upgrades. RISK: If baseline network loss is already >1%, DP5 alone cannot meet acceptance criteria; infrastructure remediation is required first.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability such that critical control commands are delivered with ≤1% packet loss and sensor feedback latency remains within [X ms] under agreed network conditions (defined as: baseline loss rate [Y]%, latency [Z±W ms], bandwidth [B Mbps]). The solution shall employ packet prioritization, state caching, and adaptive codec selection to achieve this target without requiring infrastructure changes. Acceptance criteria: (1) Measured packet loss ≤1% for critical commands under agreed conditions; (2) Sensor feedback latency ≤[X] ms (p95); (3) Graceful degradation of non-critical streams when network degrades beyond agreed bounds; (4) No violation of worksite safety policies or operational interlocks.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Manages real-time command/feedback loops, network adaptation, and state caching for remote vehicle control operations

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for QoS tagging, codec adaptation feedback, and network condition monitoring

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission parameters; prioritization logic applies QoS tags based on task criticality

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state and sensor data with adaptive codec selection based on network conditions

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Critical safety states cached locally; safety alerts trigger highest QoS priority tagging

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong fit: packet prioritization, codec adaptation, and network-aware caching are core teleoperation concerns. Diffusion occurs only at Safety Services boundary (SafetyAlert interface) for critical state caching.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns actuators and sensors, not network adaptation logic. QoS tagging and codec selection are teleoperation-layer concerns, not vehicle-layer. High diffusion to Teleoperation and Network Link.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety policy and alerts, but packet prioritization and codec adaptation are not safety-specific concerns. Caching of critical control states has safety relevance but is secondary to teleoperation's primary ownership.

Moderate fit: NGHE is a top-level container. While it could logically hold all three sub-concerns, it is overly broad and lacks specificity. Better to assign to Teleoperation (a child/peer) for clearer ownership and interface clarity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because: (1) packet prioritization and codec adaptation are intrinsic to managing real-time command/feedback over variable networks; (2) local caching of control states supports low-latency teleoperation decision-making; (3) network condition awareness is a core teleoperation concern; (4) the module already owns the Tasking and Telemetry interfaces, which feed prioritization and adaptation logic.
containment_risks: Critical safety state caching may require coordination with Safety Services; recommend SafetyAlert interface to signal when safety-critical states must be cached with highest priority.
Codec adaptation logic may need to reference vehicle capability constraints (e.g., sensor resolution limits); recommend Telemetry interface to pull vehicle metadata, avoiding tight coupling.
Network condition monitoring (e.g., latency, packet loss) must be sourced from Network Link; ensure Network Link interface provides sufficient telemetry for adaptation decisions.

If QoS tagging is enforced at a lower network layer (e.g., in Vehicle or a dedicated network stack), diffusion risk increases; clarify whether QoS tagging is a Teleoperation responsibility or delegated.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP5 is well-aligned with Teleoperation as stated. No revision needed. However, to reduce diffusion risk, consider splitting DP5 into two tightly-scoped DPs: (1) DP5a: Implement QoS tagging and packet prioritization based on task criticality and safety alerts (owned by Teleoperation); (2) DP5b: Implement network condition-aware codec adaptation with local caching of critical control states (owned by Teleoperation). This separation clarifies ownership and reduces the risk of diffusion to Vehicle or Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses the FR by proposing a hybrid approach (QoS tagging, local caching, codec adaptation) that targets the packet loss threshold constraint (≤1% or customer-defined). The DP is solution-oriented and logically sound for maintaining teleoperation reliability under variable network conditions. No logical gaps detected between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert interface boundary: Critical safety state caching bridges Teleoperation and Safety Services; requires explicit coordination protocol to ensure safety-critical states are cached with highest QoS priority without duplicating safety logic.

Vehicle metadata coupling: Codec adaptation logic may need to reference vehicle sensor resolution and capability constraints; recommend read-only Telemetry interface pull to avoid tight coupling and diffusion to Vehicle module.

Network Link dependency: QoS tagging and network condition monitoring depend on Network Link interface providing sufficient telemetry (latency, packet loss, jitter); if Network Link interface is incomplete, adaptation decisions will be degraded.

QoS tagging layer ambiguity: If QoS tagging enforcement is delegated to a lower network stack (Vehicle or dedicated network layer), diffusion risk increases; ownership must be clarified and documented in interface contracts.

Codec adaptation scope creep: If codec selection logic expands to include vehicle-specific encoding constraints or sensor-specific compression, diffusion to Vehicle module increases; recommend strict separation of concerns via interface contracts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner with strong containment score (0.85). Packet prioritization, codec adaptation, and network-aware caching are intrinsic teleoperation concerns. Diffusion is minimal (0.15) and localized to SafetyAlert boundary. Vehicle module (0.35 containment, 0.65 diffusion) and Safety Services (0.4 containment, 0.6 diffusion) are poor fits and should not own DP5 logic. NGHE Solution (0.5 containment, 0.5 diffusion) is too broad; Teleoperation is the appropriate child-level owner. Recommend formalizing interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) to enforce module boundaries and prevent diffusion creep.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions) is measurable and testable. Verification requires: (1) network simulation or field testing under defined packet loss conditions; (2) instrumentation of Teleoperation module to log QoS tagging decisions, codec selections, and cache hit rates; (3) end-to-end teleoperation latency and command fidelity metrics. Feasibility is high if network conditions are well-defined and reproducible. Recommend defining 'agreed network conditions' explicitly (e.g., latency range, packet loss distribution, bandwidth constraints) in acceptance criteria to enable repeatable verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns DP5 implementation. External dependencies are well-defined via four interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert). However, allocation clarity could be improved by: (1) explicitly documenting which sub-component within Teleoperation owns each aspect (QoS tagging logic, codec adaptation engine, cache manager); (2) defining interface contracts (data schemas, timing requirements, error handling) for each external interface; (3) clarifying whether Network Link interface provides raw network metrics or pre-processed condition assessments; (4) documenting SafetyAlert protocol for triggering highest-priority caching. Recommend creating a detailed interface specification document and internal module decomposition diagram.

recommended_revisions: Split DP5 into two tightly-scoped design parameters: (1) DP5a: Implement QoS tagging and packet prioritization based on task criticality and safety alert signals; (2) DP5b: Implement network condition-aware codec adaptation with local caching of critical control states. This separation clarifies ownership boundaries and reduces diffusion risk.

Formalize interface contracts for all four external interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) with explicit data schemas, timing requirements, and error handling protocols. Document whether Network Link provides raw metrics or pre-processed network condition assessments.

Explicitly define 'agreed network conditions' in acceptance criteria (e.g., latency range 50-500ms, packet loss distribution, bandwidth constraints, jitter limits) to enable repeatable verification and reduce ambiguity in packet loss threshold testing.

Create internal module decomposition diagram showing how DP5 is realized within Teleoperation (e.g., QoS Tagger, Codec Adapter, Cache Manager sub-components) and their internal interfaces. This clarifies ownership and reduces diffusion risk.

Document SafetyAlert coordination protocol: specify when critical control states must be cached, how safety alerts trigger highest-priority QoS tagging, and how Teleoperation and Safety Services coordinate without duplicating safety logic.

Add verification plan specifying: (1) network simulation test scenarios; (2) instrumentation points for logging QoS decisions, codec selections, cache metrics; (3) end-to-end latency and command fidelity measurement methods; (4) acceptance test procedures for packet loss ≤1% criterion under defined network conditions.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is well-addressed by DP5, the DP is architecturally sound with Teleoperation as the natural owner (0.85 containment), and verification is feasible. However, the gate analysis identifies critical diffusion risks and allocation clarity gaps that warrant decomposition before implementation. The recommended revisions—particularly splitting DP5 into two tightly-scoped sub-DPs (QoS tagging/prioritization and codec adaptation/caching) and formalizing interface contracts—are best addressed through next-level functional decomposition. This allows sub-FRs to be derived with explicit ownership boundaries, reducing diffusion creep and improving verification traceability. Given high safety criticality, decomposition is prudent to ensure each sub-FR has clear acceptance criteria and interface contracts.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Implement QoS tagging and packet prioritization based on task criticality and safety alert signals to ensure critical teleoperation commands receive highest network priority under variable network conditions.

FR5.2: Adapt codec selection and compression parameters dynamically based on real-time network condition assessment (latency, packet loss, jitter) to maintain command fidelity within agreed network conditions.

FR5.3: Cache critical control states (safety-critical commands, vehicle state snapshots) locally with highest QoS priority and coordinate cache invalidation with Safety Services to prevent stale safety state propagation.

FR5.4: Monitor and assess network link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, jitter, bandwidth) in real-time and expose condition assessments via Network Link interface to enable QoS and codec adaptation decisions.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but overly broad. Splitting into DP5a (QoS tagging/prioritization), DP5b (codec adaptation/caching), DP5c (safety state coordination), and DP5d (network monitoring) will clarify ownership boundaries, reduce diffusion risk, and enable tighter interface contracts. Each sub-DP should explicitly define: (1) which module owns the logic; (2) which external interfaces it depends on; (3) error handling and fallback behavior; (4) acceptance criteria tied to measurable network conditions.

assumptions: Network Link interface will provide sufficient telemetry (latency, packet loss, jitter) to enable real-time network condition assessment; if raw metrics are unavailable, codec adaptation decisions will be degraded.
Safety Services module will provide SafetyAlert signals with sufficient lead time to allow Teleoperation to trigger highest-priority caching before critical state transitions.
Vehicle module will expose read-only Telemetry interface (sensor resolution, capability constraints) without requiring tight coupling to codec adaptation logic.

'Agreed network conditions' can be explicitly defined and reproducibly tested (e.g., latency 50-500ms, packet loss ≤5%, jitter ≤100ms) to enable repeatable verification of ≤1% packet loss acceptance criterion.
Teleoperation module has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time codec adaptation and QoS tagging without introducing unacceptable latency overhead.
open_risks: SafetyAlert interface boundary: Critical safety state caching bridges Teleoperation and Safety Services; requires explicit coordination protocol to ensure safety-critical states are cached with highest QoS priority without duplicating safety logic. Risk: If coordination is implicit or undocumented, safety state inconsistency may occur under network stress.

QoS tagging layer ambiguity: Ownership of QoS tagging enforcement (Teleoperation vs. Network Link vs. Vehicle stack) is unclear. Risk: If delegated to lower layers without explicit interface contract, diffusion increases and Teleoperation loses visibility into tagging decisions.

Codec adaptation scope creep: If codec selection logic expands to include vehicle-specific encoding constraints or sensor-specific compression, diffusion to Vehicle module increases. Risk: Tight coupling may prevent independent evolution of codec strategies.

Network Link interface completeness: If Network Link interface does not provide sufficient telemetry granularity (e.g., per-packet loss distribution, latency percentiles), network condition assessment will be degraded. Risk: Codec adaptation and QoS decisions will be suboptimal.

Verification reproducibility: Packet loss ≤1% criterion requires well-defined 'agreed network conditions'; if conditions are vague or non-reproducible, acceptance testing will be ambiguous. Risk: Field deployment may reveal unacceptable packet loss under real-world conditions not covered by test scenarios.
evidence_needed: Detailed interface specification for Network Link (data schemas, timing requirements, error handling) clarifying whether raw metrics or pre-processed condition assessments are provided.

SafetyAlert coordination protocol document specifying: (1) when critical control states must be cached; (2) how safety alerts trigger highest-priority QoS tagging; (3) how Teleoperation and Safety Services coordinate without duplicating safety logic.
Explicit definition of 'agreed network conditions' (latency range, packet loss distribution, bandwidth constraints, jitter limits) with justification tied to real-world teleoperation use cases.
Internal module decomposition diagram for Teleoperation showing sub-components (QoS Tagger, Codec Adapter, Cache Manager, Network Monitor) and their internal interfaces.

Verification plan specifying: (1) network simulation test scenarios covering defined network conditions; (2) instrumentation points for logging QoS decisions, codec selections, cache metrics; (3) end-to-end latency and command fidelity measurement methods; (4) acceptance test procedures for packet loss ≤1% criterion.

Feasibility analysis of codec adaptation latency overhead and computational resource requirements to confirm Teleoperation module can sustain real-time adaptation without introducing unacceptable command delays.
